mission.title: Install and Configure Kuberhealthy on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kuberhealthy is a Kubernetes operator that runs synthetic checks as pods, allowing you to continuously verify that your applications and clusters are functioning as expected. It integrates seamlessly with Prometheus for monitoring and provides a simple JSON status page for easy access to check results.

steps.title: Add the Kuberhealthy Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kuberhealthy Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kuberhealthy https://kuberhealthy.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kuberhealthy using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kuberhealthy in the `kuberhealthy` namespace (it will be created if it doesn't exist) with the following command:
```bash
helm install kuberhealthy kuberhealthy/kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy --create-namespace --version v2.7.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kuberhealthy pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuberhealthy
```

steps.title: Port-forward to access the Kuberhealthy status page
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to access the Kuberhealthy status page locally:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy 8080:80
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Kuberhealthy
steps.description:
To ensure Kuberhealthy has appropriate resource limits, edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy
```
Add the following under `spec.template.spec.containers.resources`:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kuberhealthy will show the Kuberhealthy pods running in the `kuberhealthy` namespace, and you will be able to access the Kuberhealthy status page at `http://localhost:8080`. Additionally, the resource limits will be set as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kuberhealthy https://kuberhealthy.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm install kuberhealthy kuberhealthy/kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy --create-namespace --version v2.7.1

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kuberhealthy Helm release from the cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Kuberhealthy:
```bash
kubectl delete crd kuberhealthies.kuberhealthy.com
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kuberhealthy
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the kuberhealthy namespace and verify its removal:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kuberhealthy
kubectl get namespaces | grep kuberhealthy
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Kuberhealthy configuration:
```bash
helm get values kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy > kuberhealthy-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Kuberhealthy to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kuberhealthy kuberhealthy/kuberhealthy --namespace kuberhealthy --version v2.8.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Kuberhealthy pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kuberhealthy
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Kuberhealthy pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kuberhealthy
```
Investigate the error messages and adjust configurations as needed.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Kuberhealthy status page, ensure the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kuberhealthy
```
If the service is not running, check the deployment status and logs.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If Kuberhealthy fails to start due to resource constraints, check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace kuberhealthy
```
Adjust the resource limits in the deployment if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating the Helm release is not found, verify the release name and namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace kuberhealthy
```
Ensure you are using the correct release name 'kuberhealthy'.

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
